\subsection{arithmetics module}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:module-arithmetics}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics-module}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics-label}}\index{arithmetics (module)}\index{QuadraticResiduesDigraph (class in arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics.QuadraticResiduesDigraph}}\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{arithmetics.}\sphinxbfcode{QuadraticResiduesDigraph}}{\emph{integers={[}3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19{]}}}{}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{digraphs.Digraph}}}}

The \sphinxstylestrong{Legendre} symbol \sphinxstyleemphasis{(a/p)} of any pair of non null integers \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} and \sphinxstyleemphasis{p} is:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxstylestrong{0} if \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} = 0 (mod p);

\item {} 
\sphinxstylestrong{1} if \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} is a quadratic residue in \sphinxstyleemphasis{Zp}, ie \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} in \sphinxstyleemphasis{Qp};

\item {} 
\sphinxstylestrong{-1} if \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} is a non quadratic residue unit in \sphinxstyleemphasis{Zp}, ie \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} in \sphinxstyleemphasis{Up} - \sphinxstyleemphasis{Qp}.

\end{itemize}

The Legendre symbol hence defines a bipolar valuation on pairs 
of non null integers. The \sphinxstylestrong{reciprocity theorem} of the Legendre symbol 
states that, for \sphinxstyleemphasis{p} being an odd prime, \sphinxstyleemphasis{(a/p)} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{(p/a)},
apart from those pairs \sphinxstyleemphasis{(a/p)}, where \sphinxstyleemphasis{a} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{p} = 3 (mod 4). In this case, \sphinxstyleemphasis{(a/p)} = -\sphinxstyleemphasis{(p/a)}.

We may graphically illustrate the reciprocity theorem as follows:

\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{arithmetics} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{leg} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{QuadraticResiduesDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{primesBelow}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+m+mi}{20}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Odd}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{True}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{digraphs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{n}{AsymmetricPartialDigraph}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{aleg} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{AsymmetricPartialDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{leg}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{aleg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{exportGraphViz}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{legendreAsym}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\noindent{\hspace*{\fill}\sphinxincludegraphics[width=300\sphinxpxdimen]{{legendreAsym}.png}\hspace*{\fill}}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{bezout() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics.bezout}}\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxcode{arithmetics.}\sphinxbfcode{bezout}}{\emph{a}, \emph{b}}{}
Renders d = gcd(a,b) and the
Bezout coefficient x, y such that
d = ax + by.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeFareySeries() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics.computeFareySeries}}\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxcode{arithmetics.}\sphinxbfcode{computeFareySeries}}{\emph{n=7}, \emph{AsFloats=False}, \emph{Debug=False}}{}
Renders the Farey series, ie the ordered list of positive rational fractions with positive denominator lower or equal to n. For \sphinxstyleemphasis{n} = 1, we obtain: {[}{[}0,1{]},{[}1,1{]}{]}.

\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parametrs}:
\begin{quote}

\sphinxstyleemphasis{n}: strictly positive integer (default = 7).
\sphinxstyleemphasis{AsFloats}: If True (defaut False), renders the list of approximate floats corresponding to the rational fractions.
\end{quote}

\sphinxstyleemphasis{Source}: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik, Sec. 4.5 in Concrete Mathematics 2nd Ed., Addison-Wesley 1994, pp 115-123.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computePiDecimals() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics.computePiDecimals}}\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxcode{arithmetics.}\sphinxbfcode{computePiDecimals}}{\emph{decimalWordLength=4}, \emph{nbrOfWords=600}, \emph{Comments=False}}{}
Renders at least decimalWordLenght * nbrOfWords (default: 4x600=2400) decimals of \(\pi\).
The Python transcription here recodes an original C code of unknown author (see %
\begin{footnote}[*]\sphinxAtStartFootnote
\sphinxstyleemphasis{Source:} J.-P. Delahaye “\sphinxstyleemphasis{Le fascinant nombre} \(\pi\)“, Pour la science Belin 1997 p.95.
%
\end{footnote}).

Uses the following infinite Euler series:
\begin{quote}

\(\pi = 2 * \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} [ (n !) / (1 * 3  * 5 ... * (2n+1)) ].\)
\end{quote}

The series gives a new \(\pi\) decimal after adding in average 3.32 terms.

\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{arithmetics} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{n}{computePiDecimals}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{time} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{n}{time}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t0}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n}{time}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{;}\PYG{n}{piDecimals} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{computePiDecimals}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{decimalWordLength}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{nbrOfWords}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{;}\PYG{n}{t1}\PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{time}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{pi = }\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{n}{piDecimals}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{0}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{.}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{n}{piDecimals}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{pi = 3.14159265358979323846264338327950288419716939937510582097494459}
\PYG{g+go}{2307816406286208998628034825342117067982148086513282306647093844609}
\PYG{g+go}{5505822317253594081284811174502841027019385211055596446229489549303}
\PYG{g+go}{8196442881097566593344612847564823378678316527120190914564856692346}
\PYG{g+go}{034861045432664821339360726024914127372458700660630}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{precision = }\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{n+nb}{str}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n+nb}{len}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{piDecimals}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{decimals}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{precision = 314 decimals}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n+nb}{print}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+si}{\PYGZpc{}.4f}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}} \PYG{o}{\PYGZpc{}} \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t1}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{t0}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{o}{+}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{ sec.}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{0.0338 sec.}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{invSternBrocot() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}

\index{moebius\_mu() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:arithmetics.moebius_mu}}\pysiglinewithargsret{\sphinxcode{arithmetics.}\sphinxbfcode{moebius\_mu}}{\emph{n}}{}
Implements the Moebius mu function on N based on n’s prime factorization:
n = p1\textasciicircum{}e1 * … * pk\textasciicircum{}ek with each ei \textgreater{}= 1 for i = 1, …, k.

mu = 0 if ei \textgreater{} 1 for some i = 1, … k else mu = (-1)\textasciicircum{}k.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{pollard\_brent() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}

\sphinxurl{https://comeoncodeon.wordpress.com/2010/09/18/pollard-rho-brent-integer-factorization/}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{sternBrocot() (in module arithmetics)}

\begin{fulllineitems}

Renders the Stern-Brocot representation of the rational m/n (m and n are positive integers).
For instance, sternBrocot(5,7) = {[}‘L’,’R’,’R’,’L’{]}.

\sphinxstyleemphasis{Source}: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik, Sec. 4.5 in Concrete Mathematics 2nd Ed., Addison-Wesley 1994, pp 115-123.

\end{fulllineitems}

\end{fulllineitems}

\subsection{Cythonized modules for big digraphs}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cythonized-modules-for-big-digraphs}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cythonized-label}}
The following modules are compiled C-extensions using the Cython pre-compiler. No Python code source is provided for inspection. To distinguish them from the corresponding pure Python modules, a c- prefix is used.


\subsubsection{cRandPerfTabs module}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:module-cRandPerfTabs}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:crandperftabs-label}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:crandperftabs-module}}\index{cRandPerfTabs (module)}
c-Extension for the Digraph3 collection.
Module cRandPerfTabs.py is a c-compiled version of the
{\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:module-randomPerfTabs}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{randomPerfTabs}}}} module for generating random performance tableaux of Big Data type,
ie with integer action keys and float performance evaluations.

Conversions methods are provided
to switch from the standard to the BigData format and back.

Copyright (C) 2018  Raymond Bisdorff
\index{NormalizedPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.NormalizedPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{NormalizedPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

specialsation of the cPerformanceTableau class for
constructing normalized, 0 - 100, valued PerformanceTableau
instances from a given argPerfTab instance.
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
argPerfTab=None,

\item {} 
lowValue=0.0,

\item {} 
highValue=100.0,

\item {} 
coalition=None,

\item {} 
Debug=False

\end{itemize}

\end{description}
\index{normalizeEvaluations (cRandPerfTabs.NormalizedPerformanceTableau attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.NormalizedPerformanceTableau.normalizeEvaluations}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{normalizeEvaluations}}
recode the evaluations between lowValue and highValue on all criteria.
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
lowValue=0.0,

\item {} 
highValue=100.0,

\item {} 
Debug=False

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}


\end{fulllineitems}

\index{Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

Specialization of the cPerformanceTableau
for 3 objectives: \sphinxstyleemphasis{Eco}, \sphinxstyleemphasis{Soc} and \sphinxstyleemphasis{Env}.

Each decision action is qualified at random as weak (-), fair (\textasciitilde{}) or good (+)
on each of the three objectives.
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
numberOf Actions := 20 (default),

\item {} 
number of Criteria := 13 (default),

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{weightDistribution := ‘equiobjectives’ (default)}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] ‘equisignificant’ (weights set all to 1)
\item[] ‘random’ (in the range 1 to numberOfCriteria),
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
weightScale := {[}1,numerOfCriteria{]} (random default),

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonScale := (0.0, 100.0) (default)}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] (0.0,10.0) if OrdinalScales == True,
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonThresholds := ((Ind,Ind\_slope),(Pref,Pref\_slope),(Veto,Veto\_slope)) with}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] Ind \textless{} Pref \textless{} Veto in {[}0.0,100.0{]} such that 
\item[] (Ind/100.0*span + Ind\_slope*x) \textless{} (Pref/100.0*span + Pref\_slope*x) \textless{} (Pref/100.0*span + Pref\_slope*x)
\item[] By default {[}(0.05*span,0.0),(0.10*span,0.0),(0.60*span,0.0){]} if OrdinalScales=False
\item[] By default {[}(0.1*span,0.0),(0.2*span,0.0),(0.8*span,0.0){]} otherwise
\item[] with span = commonScale{[}1{]} - commonScale{[}0{]}.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonMode := {[}‘triangular’,’variable’,0.50{]} (default), A constant mode may be provided.}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] {[}‘uniform’,’variable’,None{]}, a constant range may be provided.
\item[] {[}‘beta’,’variable’,None{]} (three alpha, beta combinations:
\item[] (5.8661,2.62203),(5.05556,5.05556) and (2.62203, 5.8661)
\item[] chosen by default for ‘good’, ‘fair’ and ‘weak’ evaluations. 
\item[] Constant parameters may be provided.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
valueDigits := 2 (default, for cardinal scales only),

\item {} 
vetoProbability := x in {]}0.0-1.0{[} (0.5 default), probability that a cardinal criterion shows a veto preference discrimination threshold.

\item {} 
missingDataProbability := x in {]}0.0-1.0{[} (0.05 default), probability that an action x criterion evaluation is missing.

\item {} 
Debug := True / False (default).

\end{itemize}

\end{description}
\index{showActions (cRandPerfTabs.Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau.showActions}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{showActions}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameter}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Alphabetic=False

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.Random3ObjectivesPerformanceTableau.showObjectives}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{showObjectives}}
\end{fulllineitems}


\end{fulllineitems}

\index{RandomCBPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.RandomCBPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{RandomCBPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

Full automatic generation of random
Cost versus Benefit oriented performance tableaux.

Parameters:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
If numberOfActions == None, a uniform random number between 10 and 31 of cheap, neutral or advantageous actions (equal 1/3 probability each type) actions is instantiated

\item {} 
If numberOfCriteria == None, a uniform random number between 5 and 21 of cost or benefit criteria (1/3 respectively 2/3 probability) is instantiated

\item {} 
weightDistribution := \{‘equiobjectives’\textbar{}’fixed’\textbar{}’random’\textbar{}’equisignificant’ (default = ‘equisignificant’)\}

\item {} 
default weightScale for ‘random’ weightDistribution is 1 - numberOfCriteria

\item {} 
commonScale parameter is obsolete. The scale of cost criteria is cardinal or ordinal (0-10) with proabailities 1/4 respectively 3/4, whereas the scale of benefit criteria is ordinal or cardinal with probabilities 2/3, respectively 1/3.

\item {} 
All cardinal criteria are evaluated with decimals between 0.0 and 100.0 wheras all ordinal criteria are evaluated with integers between 0 and 10.

\item {} 
commonThresholds is obsolete. Preference discrimination is specified as percentiles of concerned performance differences (see below).

\item {} 
CommonPercentiles = \{‘ind’:0.05, ‘pref’:0.10, {[}‘weakveto’:0.90,{]} ‘veto’:‘95\} are expressed in centiless (reversed for vetoes) and only concern cardinal criteria.

\item {} 
missingDataProbability := x in {]}0.0-1.0{[} (0.05 default), probability that an action x criterion evaluation is missing.

\end{itemize}

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{warning}{Warning:}
Minimal number of decision actions required is 3 !
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{RandomCoalitionsPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.RandomCoalitionsPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{RandomCoalitionsPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

Full automatic generation of performance tableaux with random coalitions of criteria
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
numberOf Actions := 20 (default)

\item {} 
number of Criteria := 13 (default)

\item {} 
weightDistribution := ‘equisignificant’ (default with all weights = 1.0), ‘random’, ‘fixed’ (default w\_1 = numberOfCriteria-1, w\_\{i!=1\} = 1

\item {} 
weightScale := {[}1,numerOfCriteria{]} (random default), {[}w\_1, w\_\{i!=1{]} (fixed)

\item {} 
commonScale := (0.0, 100.0) (default)

\item {} 
commonThresholds := {[}(1.0,0.0),(2.001,0.0),(8.001,0.0){]} if OrdinalSacles, {[}(0.10001*span,0),(0.20001*span,0.0),(0.80001*span,0.0){]} with span = commonScale{[}1{]} - commonScale{[}0{]}.

\item {} 
commonMode := {[}‘triangular’,50.0,0.50{]} (default), {[}‘uniform’,None,None{]}, {[}‘beta’, None,None{]} (three alpha, beta combinations (5.8661,2.62203) chosen by default for high(‘+’), medium (‘\textasciitilde{}’) and low (‘-‘) evaluations.

\item {} 
valueDigits := 2 (default, for cardinal scales only)

\item {} 
Coalitions := True (default)/False, three coalitions if True

\item {} 
VariableGenerators := True (default) / False, variable high(‘+’), medium (‘\textasciitilde{}’) or low (‘-‘) law generated evaluations.

\item {} 
OrdinalScales := True / False (default)

\item {} 
Debug := True / False (default)

\item {} 
RandomCoalitions = True / False (default) zero or more than three coalitions if Coalitions == False.

\item {} 
vetoProbability := x in {]}0.0-1.0{[} / None (default), probability that a cardinal criterion shows a veto preference discrimination threshold.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{RandomPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.RandomPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{RandomPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

Specialization of the cPerformanceTableau class for generating a temporary
random performance tableau.
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
numberOfActions := nbr of decision actions.

\item {} 
numberOfCriteria := number performance criteria.

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{weightDistribution := ‘random’ (default) \textbar{} ‘fixed’ \textbar{} ‘equisignificant’.}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] If ‘random’, weights are uniformly selected randomly
\item[] form the given weight scale;
\item[] If ‘fixed’, the weightScale must provided a corresponding weights
\item[] distribution;
\item[] If ‘equisignificant’, all criterion weights are put to unity.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
weightScale := {[}Min,Max{]} (default ={[}1,numberOfCriteria{]}.

\item {} 
IntegerWeights := True (in the BigData format)

\item {} 
commonScale := {[}Min;Max{]}; common performance measuring scales (default = {[}0;100{]})

\item {} 
commonThresholds := {[}(q0,q1),(p0,p1),(v0,v1){]}; common indifference(q), preference (p)
and considerable performance difference discrimination thresholds.

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonMode := common random distribution of random performance measurements:}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] (‘uniform’,Min,Max), uniformly distributed between min and max values. 
\item[] (‘normal’,mu,sigma), truncated Gaussion distribution. 
\item[] (‘triangular’,mode,repartition), generalized triangular distribution 
\item[] (‘beta’,mode,(alpha,beta)), by default Mode=None, alpha=beta=2.
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\item {} 
valueDigits := \textless{}integer\textgreater{}, precision of performance measurements
(2 decimal digits by default).

\end{itemize}

\item[{Code example::}] \leavevmode
\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{cRandPerfTabs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{3}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{numberOfCriteria}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{actions}
\PYG{g+go}{OrderedDict([}
\PYG{g+go}{(0: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}a1\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{(1: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}a2\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}),}
\PYG{g+go}{(3: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}a3\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{})}
\PYG{g+go}{])}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{criteria}
\PYG{g+go}{OrderedDict([}
\PYG{g+go}{(\PYGZsq{}g1\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}thresholds\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}ind\PYGZsq{} : (10.0, 0.0) ),}
\PYG{g+go}{                           \PYGZsq{}veto\PYGZsq{}: (80.0, 0.0) ),}
\PYG{g+go}{                           \PYGZsq{}pref\PYGZsq{}: (20.0, 0.0) \PYGZcb{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}scale\PYGZsq{}: (0.0, 100.0),}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}weight\PYGZsq{}: 1,}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}name\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}cRandPerfTabs.RandomPerformanceTableau() instance\PYGZsq{},}
\PYG{g+go}{            \PYGZsq{}comment\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZsq{}Arguments: weightDistribution=random;}
\PYG{g+go}{                weightScale=(1, 1); commonMode=None\PYGZsq{}\PYGZcb{}).}
\PYG{g+go}{])}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{evaluation}
\PYG{g+go}{\PYGZob{}\PYGZsq{}g01\PYGZsq{}: \PYGZob{}0: 45.95, 1: 95.17, 2: 17.47.\PYGZcb{}\PYGZcb{}}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{RandomRankPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.RandomRankPerformanceTableau}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.}\sphinxbfcode{RandomRankPerformanceTableau}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau}}}}

Specialization of the cPerformanceTableau class for generating a temporary
random performance tableau.

Random generator for multiple criteria ranked (without ties) performances of a
given number of decision actions. On each criterion,
all decision actions are hence lineraly ordered. The RandomRankPerformanceTableau class is
matching the RandomLinearVotingProfiles class (see the votingDigraphs module)
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
number of actions,

\item {} 
number of performance criteria,

\item {} 
weightDistribution := equisignificant \textbar{} random (default, see RandomPerformanceTableau)

\item {} 
weightScale := (1, 1 \textbar{} numberOfCriteria (default when random))

\item {} 
integerWeights := Boolean (True = default)

\item {} \begin{description}
\item[{commonThresholds (default) := \{}] \leavevmode
\begin{DUlineblock}{0em}
\item[] ‘ind’:(0,0),
\item[] ‘pref’:(1,0),
\item[] ‘veto’:(numberOfActions,0)
\item[] \} (default)
\end{DUlineblock}

\end{description}

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{cPerformanceTableau (class in cRandPerfTabs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}

\index{showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap (cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cRandPerfTabs.cPerformanceTableau.showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap}}
shows the html heatmap version of the performance tableau in a browser window
(see perfTabs.htmlPerformanceHeatMap() method ).

\sphinxstylestrong{Parameters}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxstyleemphasis{actionsList} and \sphinxstyleemphasis{criteriaList}, if provided,  give the possibility to show the decision alternatives, resp. criteria, in a given ordering.

\item {} 
\sphinxstyleemphasis{ndigits} = 0 may be used to show integer evaluation values.

\item {} 
If no \sphinxstyleemphasis{actionsList} is provided, the decision actions are ordered from the best to the worst. This ranking is obtained by default with the Copeland rule applied on a standard \sphinxstyleemphasis{BipolarOutrankingDigraph}. When the \sphinxstyleemphasis{SparseModel} flag is put to \sphinxstyleemphasis{True}, a sparse \sphinxstyleemphasis{PreRankedOutrankingDigraph} construction is used instead.

\item {} 
The \sphinxstyleemphasis{minimalComponentSize} allows to control the fill rate of the pre-ranked model. If \sphinxstyleemphasis{minimalComponentSize} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{n} (the number of decision actions) both the pre-ranked model will be in fact equivalent to the standard model.

\item {} 
It may interesting in some cases to use \sphinxstyleemphasis{rankingRule} = ‘NetFlows’.

\item {} 
Quantiles used for the pre-ranked decomposition are put by default to \sphinxstyleemphasis{n} (the number of decision alternatives) for \sphinxstyleemphasis{n} \textless{} 50. For larger cardinalities up to 1000, quantiles = \sphinxstyleemphasis{n} /10. For bigger performance tableaux the \sphinxstyleemphasis{quantiles} parameter may be set to a much lower value not exceeding usually 1000.

\item {} 
The pre-ranking may be obtained with three ordering strategies for the quantiles equivalence classes: ‘average’ (default), ‘optimistic’ or  ‘pessimistic’.

\item {} 
With \sphinxstyleemphasis{Correlations} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{True} and \sphinxstyleemphasis{criteriaList} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{None}, the criteria will be presented from left to right in decreasing order of the correlations between the marginal criterion based ranking and the global ranking used for presenting the decision alternatives.

\item {} 
For large performance Tableaux, \sphinxstyleemphasis{multiprocessing} techniques may be used by setting.

\item {} 
\sphinxstyleemphasis{Threading} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{True} in order to speed up the computations; especially when \sphinxstyleemphasis{Correlations} = \sphinxstyleemphasis{True}.

\item {} 
By default, the number of cores available, will be detected. It may be necessary in a HPC context to indicate the exact number of singled threaded cores that are actually allocated to the running job.

\end{itemize}

\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{cRandomPerfTabs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rt} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{seed}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{100}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{rt}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showHTMLPerformanceHeatmap}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{colorLevels}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{5}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{Correlations}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\noindent{\hspace*{\fill}\sphinxincludegraphics[width=600\sphinxpxdimen]{{perfTabsExample}.png}\hspace*{\fill}}

\end{fulllineitems}


\end{fulllineitems}

\subsubsection{cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs module}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:module-cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cintegeroutrankingdigraphs-label}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cintegeroutrankingdigraphs-module}}\index{cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs (module)}
c-Extension for the Digraph3 collection.
Module cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.py is a c-compiled part of the
{\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:module-outrankingDigraphs}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{outrankingDigraphs}}}} module for handling random performance tableaux of Big Data type,
ie with integer action keys and float performance evaluations.

Copyright (C) 2018  Raymond Bisdorff
\index{IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph (class in cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.}\sphinxbfcode{IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:outrankingDigraphs.BipolarOutrankingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{outrankingDigraphs.BipolarOutrankingDigraph}}}}, {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:perfTabs.PerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{perfTabs.PerformanceTableau}}}}

Specialization of the abstract OutrankingDigraph root class for generating
integer-valued bipolar outranking digraphs.
\begin{description}
\item[{Parameters:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
argPerfTab: instance of PerformanceTableau class.
If a file name string is given, the performance tableau will directly be loaded first.

\item {} 
coalition: subset of criteria to be used for contruction the outranking digraph.

\item {} 
hasNoVeto: veto desactivation flag (False by default).

\item {} 
hasBipolarVeto: bipolar versus electre veto activation (true by default).

\item {} 
Threading: False by default. Allows to profit from SMP machines via the Python multiprocessing module.

\item {} 
nbrCores: controls the maximal number of cores that will be used in the multiprocessing phases.
If None is given, the os.cpu\_count method is used in order to determine the number of availble cores on the SMP machine.

\end{itemize}

\item[{Example Python session:}] \leavevmode
\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]

\index{computeDeterminateness (cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

Computes the Kendalll distance in \% of self
with the all median valued (indeterminate) digraph.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrderCorrelation (cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph.computeOrderCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeOrderCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
order (ordered sequence from worst to best of action keys),

\item {} 
bint Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

wrapper for the self.computeRankingCorrelation method
The given argOrder is previously reversed.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrdinalCorrelation (cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph.computeOrdinalCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeOrdinalCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
other,

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation K of an integer Digraph instance
when compared with a given compatible (same actions set) other integer Digraph or
Digraph instance.

\sphinxstyleemphasis{Formulas}:

K = sum\_\{x != y\} {[} min( max(-self.relation(x,y)),other.relation(x,y), max(self.relation(x,y),-other.relation(x,y)) {]}

K /= sum\_\{x!=y\} {[} min(abs(self.relation(x,y),abs(other.relation(x,y)) {]}

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
The global outranking relation of BigDigraph instances is contructed on the fly
from the ordered dictionary of the components.

Renders a tuple with at position 0 the actual bipolar correlation index
and in position 1 the minimal determination level D of self and
the other relation.

D = sum\_\{x != y\} min(abs(self.relation(x,y)),abs(other.relation(x,y)) / n(n-1)

where n is the number of actions considered.

The correlation index with a completely indeterminate relation
is by convention 0.0 at determination level 0.0 .
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrdinalCorrelationMP (cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph.computeOrdinalCorrelationMP}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeOrdinalCorrelationMP}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
other (digraph instance),

\item {} 
Threading=True,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=True,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Multi processing version of the digraphs.computeOrdinalCorrelation() method.

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
The relation filtering and the MedinaCut option are not implemented in the MP version.
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingCorrelation (cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph.computeRankingCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeRankingCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
ranking (ordered sequence from best to worst of action keys),

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation K of an integer digraph instance
when compared with a given linear ranking of its actions

K = sum\_\{x != y\} {[} min( max(-self.relation(x,y)),other.relation(x,y), max(self.relation(x,y),-other.relation(x,y)) {]}

K /= sum\_\{x!=y\} {[} min(abs(self.relation(x,y),abs(other.relation(x,y)) {]}

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
The global outranking relation of BigDigraph instances is contructed on the fly
from the ordered dictionary of the components.

Renders a tuple with at position 0 the actual bipolar correlation index
and in position 1 the minimal determination level D of self and
the other relation.

D = sum\_\{x != y\} min(abs(self.relation(x,y)),abs(other.relation(x,y)) / n(n-1)

where n is the number of actions considered.

The correlation index with a completely indeterminate relation
is by convention 0.0 at determination level 0.0 .
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

Back to the {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:technical-label}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{std,std-ref}{Installation}}}}


\subsubsection{cIntegerSortingDigraphs module}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cintegersortingdigraphs-label}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cintegersortingdigraphs-module}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:module-cIntegerSortingDigraphs}}\index{cIntegerSortingDigraphs (module)}
c-Extension for the Digraph3 collection.
Module cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.py is a c-compiled part of the
{\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:module-outrankingDigraphs}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{outrankingDigraphs}}}} module for handling random performance tableaux of Big Data type,
ie with integer action keys and float performance evaluations.

Copyright (C) 2018  Raymond Bisdorff
\index{IntegerQuantilesSortingDigraph (class in cIntegerSortingDigraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerSortingDigraphs.IntegerQuantilesSortingDigraph}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cIntegerSortingDigraphs.}\sphinxbfcode{IntegerQuantilesSortingDigraph}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}}}}
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
argPerfTab=None,

\item {} 
limitingQuantiles=4,

\item {} 
LowerClosed=False,

\item {} 
PrefThresholds=True,

\item {} 
hasNoVeto=False,

\item {} 
outrankingType = “bipolar”,

\item {} 
CompleteOutranking = False,

\item {} 
StoreSorting=False,

\item {} 
CopyPerfTab=False,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
tempDir=None,

\item {} 
nbrCores=None,

\item {} 
nbrOfProcesses=None,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Cythonized c-Extension of the {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}}}} class  for the sorting of very large sets of alternatives into quantiles delimited ordered classes.

A {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.IntegerBipolarOutrankingDigraph}}}} class specialisation.

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
We generally require an PerformanceTableau instance or a valid filename.
If no limitingQuantiles parameter quantity is given, a default profile with the limiting quartiles Q0,Q1,Q2, Q3 and Q4 is used on each criteria.

By default, upper closed limits of categories are used in the sorting algorithm.
\end{sphinxadmonition}
\begin{description}
\item[{Example Python session:}] \leavevmode
\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{cRandPerfTabs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{t} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{RandomPerformanceTableau}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{numberOfActions}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{25}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{cIntegerSortingDigraphs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{o}{*}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{so} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{IntegerQuantilesSortingDigraph}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{t}\PYG{p}{,}\PYG{n}{limitingQuantiles}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{quintiles}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{so}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showSorting}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Sorting results in descending order \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.80 \PYGZhy{} 1.00]:   [10, 17, 23, 24]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.60 \PYGZhy{} 0.80]:   [0, 2, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11, 12, 17, 19, 23]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.40 \PYGZhy{} 0.60]:   [1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 12, 13, 14, 16, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.20 \PYGZhy{} 0.40]:   [7, 13, 15, 20]}
\PYG{g+go}{]\PYGZlt{} \PYGZhy{} 0.20]:      []}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{so}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showSorting}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{Reverse}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{False}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{*\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{} Sorting results in ascending order \PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}*}
\PYG{g+go}{]\PYGZlt{} \PYGZhy{} 0.20]:      []}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.20 \PYGZhy{} 0.40]:   [7, 13, 15, 20]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.40 \PYGZhy{} 0.60]:   [1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 12, 13, 14, 16, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.60 \PYGZhy{} 0.80]:   [0, 2, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11, 12, 17, 19, 23]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.80 \PYGZhy{} 1.00]:   [10, 17, 23, 24]}
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{n}{so}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{showQuantileOrdering}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{strategy}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{average}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.80\PYGZhy{}1.00] : [24]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.60\PYGZhy{}1.00] : [10, 17, 23]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.60\PYGZhy{}0.80] : [0, 4, 5, 6, 11]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.40\PYGZhy{}0.80] : [2, 12, 19]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.40\PYGZhy{}0.60] : [1, 3, 8, 9, 14, 16, 18, 21, 22]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.20\PYGZhy{}0.60] : [13, 20]}
\PYG{g+go}{]0.20\PYGZhy{}0.40] : [7, 15]}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\end{description}

\subsubsection{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs module}
\label{\detokenize{techDoc:csparseintegeroutrankingdigraphs-label}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:csparseintegeroutrankingdigraphs-module}}\label{\detokenize{techDoc:module-cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs}}\index{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs (module)}
c-Extension for the Digraph3 collection.
Module cBigIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.py is a c-compiled partial version of the
{\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:module-sparseOutrankingDigraphs}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{sparseOutrankingDigraphs}}}} module for handling outranking digraphs of very large order.

Copyright (C) 2018  Raymond Bisdorff
\index{SparseIntegerDigraph (class in cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.}\sphinxbfcode{SparseIntegerDigraph}}
Bases: \sphinxhref{https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/functions.html\#object}{\sphinxcode{object}}

Abstract root class for linearly decomposed big digraphs (order \textgreater{} 1000)
using multiprocessing ressources.
\index{computeDecompositionSummaryStatistics (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph.computeDecompositionSummaryStatistics}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeDecompositionSummaryStatistics}}
Returns the summary of the distribution of the length of
the components as follows:

\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{summary} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{max}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{maxLength}\PYG{p}{,}
           \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{median}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{n}{medianLength}\PYG{p}{,}
           \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{mean}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:}\PYG{n}{meanLength}\PYG{p}{,}
           \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{stdev}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{stdLength}\PYG{p}{,}
           \PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{l+s+s1}{fillrate}\PYG{l+s+s1}{\PYGZsq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{fillrate}\PYG{p}{,}
                      \PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{see} \PYG{n}{computeFillRate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeFillRate (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph.computeFillRate}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeFillRate}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the sum of the squares (without diagonal) of the orders of the component’s subgraphs
over the square (without diagonal) of the big digraph order.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeOrdinalCorrelation (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph.computeOrdinalCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeOrdinalCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
other (digraph instance),

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation K of a SparseDigraph instance
when compared with a given compatible (same actions set) other Digraph or
SparseDigraph instance.

K = sum\_\{x != y\} {[} min( max(-self.relation(x,y)),other.relation(x,y), max(self.relation(x,y),-other.relation(x,y)) {]}

K /= sum\_\{x!=y\} {[} min(abs(self.relation(x,y),abs(other.relation(x,y)) {]}

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
The global outranking relation of SparseDigraph instances is contructed on the fly
from the ordered dictionary of the components.

Renders a tuple with at position 0 the actual bipolar correlation index
and in position 1 the minimal determination level D of self and
the other relation.

D = sum\_\{x != y\} min(abs(self.relation(x,y)),abs(other.relation(x,y)) / n(n-1)

where n is the number of actions considered.

The correlation index with a completely indeterminate relation
is by convention 0.0 at determination level 0.0 .
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeRankingCorrelation (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph.computeRankingCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeRankingCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
ranking (ordered list from best to worst),

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation K of a SparseDigraph instance
when compared with a given linear ranking of its actions

K = sum\_\{x != y\} {[} min( max(-self.relation(x,y)),other.relation(x,y), max(self.relation(x,y),-other.relation(x,y)) {]}

K /= sum\_\{x!=y\} {[} min(abs(self.relation(x,y),abs(other.relation(x,y)) {]}

\begin{sphinxadmonition}{note}{Note:}
The global outranking relation of SparseDigraph instances is contructed on the fly
from the ordered dictionary of the components.

Renders a tuple with at position 0 the actual bipolar correlation index
and in position 1 the minimal determination level D of self and
the other relation.

D = sum\_\{x != y\} min(abs(self.relation(x,y)),abs(other.relation(x,y)) / n(n-1)

where n is the number of actions considered.

The correlation index with a completely indeterminate relation
is by convention 0.0 at determination level 0.0 .
\end{sphinxadmonition}

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{showHTMLRelationMap (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

See corresponding {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:digraphs.Digraph.showRelationMap}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{digraphs.Digraph.showRelationMap()}}}} method.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{showRelationMap (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph.showRelationMap}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{showRelationMap}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
fromIndex=0,

\item {} 
toIndex=0,

\item {} 
symbols=None.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Prints on the console, in text map format, the location of
the diagonal outranking components of the big outranking digraph.
\begin{description}
\item[{By default, symbols := \{‘max’:’┬’,’positive’: ‘+’, ‘median’: ‘ ‘,}] \leavevmode
‘negative’: ‘-‘, ‘min’: ‘┴’\}

\end{description}

The default ordering of the output is following the quantiles sorted boosted net flows ranking rule
from best to worst actions. Further available ranking rules are Kohler’s (rankingRule=”kohler”)
and Tideman’s ranked pairs rule (rankingRule=”rankedPairs”).

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph (class in cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.}\sphinxbfcode{SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerDigraph}}}}, {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:perfTabs.PerformanceTableau}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{perfTabs.PerformanceTableau}}}}
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
argPerfTab,

\item {} 
quantiles=4,

\item {} 
quantilesOrderingStrategy=”average”,

\item {} 
LowerClosed=False,

\item {} 
componentRankingRule=”Copeland”,

\item {} 
minimalComponentSize=1,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
tempDir=None,

\item {} 
componentThreadingThreshold=1000,        * nbrOfCPUs=1,

\item {} 
save2File=None,

\item {} 
CopyPerfTab=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Main class for the multiprocessing implementation of big outranking digraphs.

The big outranking digraph instance is decomposed with a q-tiling sort into a partition
of quantile equivalence classes which are linearly ordered by average quantile limits (default).

With each quantile equivalence class is associated a BipolarOutrankingDigraph object
which is restricted to the decision actions gathered in this quantile equivalence class.

By default, the number of quantiles q is set to quartiles. However, the ranking quality and the best choice results get better with a finer grained quantiles decomposition.

For other parameters settings, see the corresponding {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}}}} class.

Example python3.6 session:

\index{computeActionCategories (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeActionCategories}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeActionCategories}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
action (int key),

\item {} 
Show=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=1.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the union of categories in which the given action is sorted positively or null into.
Returns a tuple : action, lowest category key, highest category key, membership credibility !

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeBoostedOrdering (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeBoostedOrdering}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeBoostedOrdering}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameter}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
orderingRule=’Copeland’.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders an ordred list of decision actions ranked in
increasing preference direction following by default the Copeland rule
on each component.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeBoostedRanking (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeBoostedRanking}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeBoostedRanking}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameter}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
rankingRule=’Copeland’.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders an ordred list of decision actions ranked in
decreasing preference direction following the net flows rule
on each component.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
criterion,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=1,

\item {} 
Debug=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation coefficient between
the global outranking and the marginal criterion relation.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeDeterminateness (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeDeterminateness}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeDeterminateness}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameter}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
InPercent=True.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Computes the Kendalll distance in \% of self
with the all median valued (indeterminate) digraph.

deter = (sum\_\{x,y in X\} abs{[}r(xSy) - Med{]})/(oder*order-1)

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph.computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Sorted=True,

\item {} 
ValuedCorrelation=False,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrCores=None,

\item {} 
Comments=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Method for computing correlations between each individual criterion relation with the corresponding
global outranking relation.

Returns a list of tuples (correlation,criterionKey) sorted by default in decreasing order of the correlation.

If Threading is True, a multiprocessing Pool class is used with a parallel equivalent of the built-in map function.

If nbrCores is not set, the os.cpu\_count() function is used to determine the number of
available cores.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{showActions (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph attribute)}

\index{cQuantilesRankingDigraph (class in cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{class }\sphinxcode{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.}\sphinxbfcode{cQuantilesRankingDigraph}}
Bases: {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.SparseIntegerOutrankingDigraph}}}}
\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
argPerfTab,

\item {} 
quantiles=4,

\item {} 
quantilesOrderingStrategy=”average”,

\item {} 
LowerClosed=False,

\item {} 
componentRankingRule=”Copeland”,

\item {} 
minimalComponentSize=1,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
tempDir=None,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=1,

\item {} 
save2File=None,

\item {} 
CopyPerfTab=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Cythonized class for the multiprocessing implementation of multiple criteria quantiles ranking of very big performance tableaux - \textgreater{} 100000.

By default, the number of quantiles q is set to quartiles. However, the ranking quality gets better with a finer grained quantiles decomposition.

For other parameters settings, see the corresponding {\hyperref[\detokenize{techDoc:sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{sortingDigraphs.QuantilesSortingDigraph}}}} class.

Example python3.6 session:

\fvset{hllines={, ,}}%
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{g+gp}{\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{}\PYGZgt{} }\PYG{k+kn}{from} \PYG{n+nn}{cRandPerfTabs} \PYG{k}{import} \PYG{o}{*}

\PYG{g+go}{                                    Threading=True,seed=100)}

\index{computeActionCategories (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph.computeActionCategories}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeActionCategories}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
action (int key),

\item {} 
Show=False,

\item {} 
Debug=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=1.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the union of categories in which the given action is sorted positively or null into.
Returns a tuple : action, lowest category key, highest category key, membership credibility !

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph.computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeCriterion2RankingCorrelation}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
criterion,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrOfCPUs=1,

\item {} 
Debug=False,

\item {} 
Comments=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Renders the ordinal correlation coefficient between
the global outranking and the marginal criterion relation.

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeDeterminateness (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph.computeDeterminateness}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeDeterminateness}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameter}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
InPercent=True.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Computes the Kendalll distance in \% of self
with the all median valued (indeterminate) digraph.

deter = (sum\_\{x,y in X\} abs{[}r(xSy) - Med{]})/(oder*order-1)

\end{fulllineitems}

\index{computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations (cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph attribute)}

\begin{fulllineitems}
\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{techDoc:cSparseIntegerOutrankingDigraphs.cQuantilesRankingDigraph.computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations}}\pysigline{\sphinxbfcode{computeMarginalVersusGlobalOutrankingCorrelations}}~\begin{description}
\item[{\sphinxstyleemphasis{Parameters}:}] \leavevmode\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
Sorted=True,

\item {} 
ValuedCorrelation=False,

\item {} 
Threading=False,

\item {} 
nbrCores=None,

\item {} 
Comments=False.

\end{itemize}

\end{description}

Method for computing correlations between each individual criterion relation with the corresponding
global outranking relation.

Returns a list of tuples (correlation,criterionKey) sorted by default in decreasing order of the correlation.

If Threading is True, a multiprocessing Pool class is used with a parallel equivalent of the built-in map function.

If nbrCores is not set, the os.cpu\_count() function is used to determine the number of
available cores.

\end{fulllineitems}

\begin{fulllineitems}

\end{description}

\end{fulllineitems}
